title: Coffee to Water Ratio Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate exact coffee and water amounts for drip, French press, AeroPress, espresso, and cold brew using SCA gold cup standards. Results in grams and oz.
intro: Getting your coffee-to-water ratio right is the single biggest factor in brew quality. Too little coffee produces weak, sour cups; too much creates harsh, bitter ones. This calculator applies the **SCA Gold Cup Standard** (1:15–1:18 for filter coffee) and method-specific ratios to give you exact doses in grams and fluid ounces — no guesswork required.
keyTakeaway: For a standard 12 oz drip cup at a 1:16 ratio, you need 21 g of coffee and 340 g (12 oz) of water — most home brewers use 30–40% too little coffee.
useCases: Scaling up a drip brewer from 2 cups to a full 12-cup carafe
Dialing in a French press for a single large mug
Calculating coffee dose for AeroPress travel brewing
Measuring espresso yield to hit a 1:2 brew ratio
Mixing a large cold brew concentrate batch for the week
Comparing how much coffee each brew method actually uses
explanation:
## How the Ratio Works

Coffee strength is determined by the **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** in your cup, which depends directly on the coffee-to-water ratio. The Specialty Coffee Association (SCA) defines the Gold Cup Standard for brewed filter coffee as a TDS of 1.15–1.35%, achieved with a brew ratio of approximately **1:15 to 1:18** (coffee:water by weight).

All ratios are expressed as **1 part coffee : X parts water by mass (grams)**.

## Ratios Used by Method

| Method | Ratio | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Drip / Pour Over | 1:16 | SCA Gold Cup center point |
| French Press | 1:15 | Immersion; slightly stronger to compensate for sediment |
| AeroPress | 1:14 | Short contact time; concentrated base |
| Espresso | 1:2 | High-pressure extraction; 18 g in → 36 g out |
| Cold Brew Concentrate | 1:8 | 12–24 h steep; dilute 1:1 before drinking |

## Formula

```
total_water_g = serving_size_oz × 29.5735 × cups
coffee_g     = total_water_g / ratio
```

Where `ratio` is the X in 1:X from the table above.

**1 fl oz = 29.5735 ml = 29.5735 g** (water at room temperature; density ≈ 1 g/ml).

## Worked Example — Drip, 2 × 8 oz cups

1. Total water = 8 oz × 29.5735 × 2 = **473.2 g**
2. Ratio = 16 → Coffee = 473.2 / 16 = **29.6 g**
3. In tablespoons: 29.6 g ÷ 5.3 g/tbsp ≈ **5.6 tbsp**

## Grind Size Guide

- **Extra Fine** — Turkish (not covered here)
- **Fine** — Espresso (200–400 µm)
- **Medium-Fine** — AeroPress, pour over cone (400–600 µm)
- **Medium** — Flat-bed drip / Chemex (600–800 µm)
- **Coarse** — French Press, cold brew (800–1000 µm)

## Limitations

- Ratios are starting points. Roast level (light roasts often need less water), bean origin, grind uniformity, and water temperature (195–205 °F / 91–96 °C optimal) all affect final taste.
- Espresso yield varies by machine pressure (typically 9 bar) and portafilter size. This calculator uses a standard 18 g dose; adjust for your basket.
- Cold brew concentrate should be diluted approximately 1:1 with water or milk before drinking — the output reflects the concentrate batch only.
- 1 tablespoon of ground coffee weighs approximately 5–6 g depending on grind size and roast; use a scale for precision.

faq.q: What is the SCA Gold Cup Standard?
faq.a: The Specialty Coffee Association Gold Cup Standard defines ideal brewed coffee as having 1.15–1.35% Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) and an extraction yield of 18–22%. For home drip brewers, this translates to roughly 55 g of coffee per liter of water (1:18) to 63 g/L (1:15.9), with 1:16–1:17 as the practical sweet spot.

faq.q: Why does espresso use a 1:2 ratio when drip uses 1:16?
faq.a: Espresso forces near-boiling water through finely ground coffee at 9 bar of pressure in 25–30 seconds. The intense pressure extracts a highly concentrated shot (~36 g output from 18 g coffee). Drip uses gravity and a much longer contact time with more water, so it needs a lower coffee concentration to achieve similar TDS in the final cup.

faq.q: Should I measure coffee by weight or volume (scoops/tablespoons)?
faq.a: Always measure by weight when possible. Ground coffee density varies by roast level and grind size — a tablespoon can range from 4 g to 7 g. A $10–15 kitchen scale removes that variable entirely and is the single best upgrade for consistent brewing.

faq.q: How much coffee do I need for a standard 12-cup drip maker?
faq.a: A '12-cup' US drip maker typically holds 60 fl oz (1,775 ml) at full capacity. At a 1:16 ratio that is 111 g of coffee, or roughly 21 tablespoons. Most drip maker scoops are marked for a weaker ratio — ignore them and weigh your dose.

faq.q: Does water quality affect the ratio?
faq.a: Yes. The SCA recommends water with 75–250 mg/L total dissolved minerals (moderately hard). Very soft water (below 50 mg/L) under-extracts and tastes flat; very hard water (above 300 mg/L) over-extracts and tastes harsh. If your tap water is heavily chlorinated or very hard, filtered or third-wave water improves consistency more than tweaking the ratio.

faq.q: What ratio should I start with for AeroPress?
faq.a: 1:14 is a good starting point for a concentrated cup that mimics a short espresso-style shot. For a full 8 oz mug brewed directly, try 1:12 to 1:15 with a medium-fine grind and a 2-minute steep. AeroPress is highly forgiving — the inverted method at 1:13 with 1 minute steep is popular in competition settings.

faq.q: Is cold brew at 1:8 too strong to drink straight?
faq.a: Yes — cold brew concentrate at 1:8 is typically 2× to 2.5× the strength of regular drip coffee. Most people dilute it 1:1 with water or milk before drinking. If you prefer ready-to-drink cold brew, use a 1:14 to 1:16 ratio and steep for the same 12–24 hours.

faq.q: How does grind size interact with ratio?
faq.a: Grind size controls extraction rate. A coarser grind has less surface area and extracts more slowly — so immersion methods like French press use coarse grinds with slightly richer ratios (1:15) to compensate. Finer grinds increase surface area and extraction speed, which is why espresso uses fine grinds with a very short brew time. Changing grind without adjusting ratio shifts TDS and can push your cup outside the Gold Cup range.

faq.q: Can I use this for a moka pot?
faq.a: Moka pot ratios depend entirely on the size of your pot (1-cup through 12-cup models); the chamber dictates water volume and the filter basket dictates coffee volume. A typical 3-cup moka pot uses about 150 ml water and 15–17 g of coffee (roughly 1:9 to 1:10). The output is strong espresso-style coffee, not true espresso. Fill the basket level — do not tamp.
